What   wi l l   we  cover   t oday?

Information Ethics
Why all the fuss?

5
       What   i s  et hi cs?


       Ethics is the study and practice of the
       principles that govern the behavior of people


       Information ethics (or cyber ethics) focuses on
       the moral issues arising from the development
       and use of information systems


       Key aspects or focus areas include:
       privacy, accountability, consent, accuracy,
       authority, accessibility, availability and
       ambiguity, intellectual property, pirating, and
       copyright
















       https://www.123rf.com/photo_16633536_abstract-word-cloud-for-inform ation-ethics-with-related-tags-
       and-term s.htm l                                                                                                                                       5

Why  i s  i t   i mpor t ant   t o  st udy  et hi cs?


Let ’s hear from Big Tech…


Google: $22.5 million fine was the largest fine paid so far, from a 2012 commission finding that
the company misrepresented to users of the Safari Internet browser that it would not place
tracking “cookies” or serve targeted ads to those users


Cambridge Analytica: clients included Donald Trump's 2016 presidential campaign, sought
information on Facebook users to build psychological profiles on a large portion of the U.S.
electorate.


Tw i t t e r: blocked people from posting and forwarding a legitimate newspaper report to its
re ad e r s   and   f ol l ow e r s








                                                                                                                                            6

What   ar e  some  speci fi c  exampl es?


Banks   rat e   cus t ome r s   and   as s e s s   cre d i t
worthiness. Issues: customers treated as statistical
entities – service is de-personalised       and de-
contextualised


Credit  card organisations     access customer profiles.
Issues: risk of abuse & data theft.


Insurance   companies accessing private dat.
Example: Using private medical data to increase
customer premiums.


Digital music/movies copied/shared. Issues: Piracy
vs. convenience and easy access






                                                                                                                                        7

Who  or   what   i s  r esponsi bl e?


It’s the people, not computers!


People  copy, use and distribute software


People  search organisational     databases for sensitive and personal information


People  create and spread viruses


People  hack into computer systems to steal information


People  (employees) destroy and steal information


…so, how do we help (coerce?) to ensure ethical conduct?

What   ar e  et hi cal   di l emmas  or   confl i ct s?


This is where a conflict       ex i st s  b e t we e n  t wo  moral
imperatives


These can lead to the most challenging decisions in your
professional (and personal) lives


Examples:
Publishing sensitive government data for awareness vs.
committing a crime
Making all data public for transparency vs. protect
personal data for privacy
Scaled Digital services provision vs. widening digital divide













https://nymag.com/intelligencer/2016/08/trolley-problem-meme-tum blr-philosophy.html
                                                                                                                                                     9

Laws, Codes and
Policies

Part of the ethical conduct toolbox

What   ar e  codes  of  et hi cs?


Wri tten guidelines           issued by a body to its members                     to help them
conduct themselves ethically


For example, the Australia Computer Society states the following
values


The Primacy of the Public Interest: You will place the interests of the public above those of persona, business or sectional interests
The Enhancement of Quality of Life: You will strive to enhance the quality of life of those affected by your work
Honesty: You will be honest in your representation of skills, knowledge, services and products.
Competence: You will work competently and diligently for your stakeholders
Professional Development: You will enhance your own professional development, and that of your colleagues and staff
Professionalism: You will enhance the integrity of the Society and the respect of its members for each other







                                                                                                                                                                               11

What   about   ot her   exampl es?











































                                                                                               12
                                                                                                12

     What   ar e  l aws  and  r egul at i ons?


     Laws vs Ethics
     Laws: rules that mandate or prohibit certain societal behaviour
     Ethics: define socially acceptable behaviour
     Laws carry sanctions of a governing authority; ethics may not


     Incentives vs Deterrents
     What are the incentives for adhering to the Law ? For adhering to Ethics?
     What are the deterrent for breaking the Law ? For violating Ethics?
     Who/what are the enforcers?









Whitman & Mattord        (2005)                                                                                                          13

14

      What   t ypes  of  l aws  ar e  t her e?


      In Australia there are two main types of law:

      Case law or common law                                                                    The job of an information systems
      These are the decisions of judges in the courts –          re l at i ng   t o             professional is to have an
      specific disputes                                                                         understanding of the legal
                                                                                                framework within which they and
                                                                                                their organisation operates under
      Legislation
      The laws made by the Federal and State parliaments. There                                 There are specialised lawyers for
      are hundreds of specific laws at each level. There are specific                           technology and information related
      Acts (or legislation) that deal with various information                                  issues (this is what Law at
      systems issues                                                                            Swinburne focuses on)

15

      What   about   pol i cy?  How  i s  i t   di ffer ent   t o  l aw?


      Policies are guidelines     that describe acceptable and unacceptable employee behaviours in the
      workplace


      Policies can also function an ‘internal laws’ with complete with penalties, judicial practices, and
      sanctions to require compliance


      The difference between a policy and a law, however, is that ignorance of a policy is an acceptable
      defence


      Policies do not necessarily have allocated re s ource s         and people for administration and
      enforcement

When  does  pol i cy  become  enfor ceabl e?


Thus, for a policy to become enforceable, it must meet the following five criteria:


Dissemination    (distribution) –  made readily available for review by the all those effected.


Review  (reading) –   In an intelligible format, including versions for illiterate, non-English reading,
and reading-impaired employees.


Comprehension      (understanding) -    must be able to demonstrate that the employee understood
the requirements


Compliance    (agreement) -    must be able to demonstrate that the employee agreed to comply with
the policy through act or affirmation


Uniform   enforcement -    demonstrate that the policy has been uniformly enforced, regardless of
employee status or assignment

                                                                                                                                  16

What   I T  pol i ci es  do  or gani zat i ons  mai nt ai n?


Organisations     strive to build a corporate culture based on ethical principles and policies that
employees can understand and implement


These policies typically include:
Ethical computer use policy
Information privacy policy
Email privacy policy
Acceptable use policy
Internet use policy
Social media policy
Wo r k p l a c e   mo n i t o r i n g   p o l i c y


Can you find the Swinburne policies on the above issues?




                                                                                                                                          17

Information Privacy
Profiling and Protection

What   i s  pr i vacy?  Why  so  i mpor t ant ?


Privacy is having the power to seclude                         oneself, or information about oneself, in order to limit the
influence       others can have on our behaviour


Tr  a   d   i   t   i   o   n   a   l   l   y       r  e   c   o   g   n   i   z   e   d       a   s       a       p   r  e   r  e   q   u   i   s   i   t   e       f   o   r       t   h   e       e  x  e   r  c   i   s   e       o   f    human rights such as the freedom of
ex p re ssi ons,   t he   fre e d om  of  associ at i on,   and   t he   fre e d om  of  choi ce


One of the hottest             topics in information security


Many organisations               are collecting, swapping, and selling personal information                                      as a commodity


Many people are looking to governments for protection                                       of their privacy







                                                                                                                                                                                     19

Does Australi a have a pri vacy act?


In short, yes, but, there is a whole set of tools:


In Australia the issues and laws relating to privacy are covered in the Information Privacy
Principles (IPPs)


The Privacy Act 1988 (Privacy Act) is         the principal piece of Australian legislation protecting the
handling of personal information about individuals. This includes the collection, use, storage and
disclosure of personal information in the federal public sector and in the private sector.


There also exists the: Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010. It provides that the
Australian Information Commissioner has the function of reporting to the Attorney-General. It
guides how public sector information is: collected, used, disclosed, managed, administered,
stored, accessed


There are more…





https://www.ag.gov.au/rights-and-protections/privacy
                                                                                                                                               20

21

What   i s  di gi t al   pr ofi l i ng,   fi l t er i ng  and  sur vei l l ance?


Profiling   is the process of creating a digital dossier. A digital
dossier is an electronic description of you and your habits.


Aggregators      are companies that collect public data such as
re al   e s t at e   re cord s ,   t e l e p hone   numb e r s ,   and   non-public
data such as social security numbers, financial data, police
re cord s ,   mot or   v e hi cl e   re cord s   and   i nt e g rat e   t he m  t o
produce digital dossiers


Amazon     filed a patent application for an algorithm that
would let future versions of the device identify statements
of interest. Enabling the speaker to be monitored based on
their interests and targeted for related advertising.









                                                                                                                                                     22

What   about   AI ?


AI brings the ability to gather, analyse, and combine vast quantities of data from different
sources –   to eventually self learn


The potential impact of AI on privacy is immense: data exploitation; identification and tracking;
prediction; profiling


AI can also use information as input for the purpose of sorting, scoring classifying, evaluating and
ranki ng   p e op l e

Cybersecurity and
Crime
Scope, Scale and Solutions

What   i s  cyber   cr i me?

























                                                                                                       25

What   i s  t he  st at us  of  cyber   cr i me?


                          Reports in USA 2001-21                                                                                   Ty p e s  o f a tta c k s  i n  A u s tra l i a  2 0 2 1















































https://www.cyber.gov.au/acsc/view-all-content/reports-and-statistics/acsc-annual-cyber-threat-report-2020-21

https://www.statista.com/statistics/267132/total-damage-caused-by-by-cyber-crime-in-the-us/
                                                                                                                                                                                                                26

How i s cyber cri me categori sed i n Austral i a?


Level 1 Accessing data or impairing an electronic communication if this is
done with the intention to commit a serious offence.


Level 2 Impairing or modifying data, or impairing electronic
communications, if done with the intent to cause (or with recklessness to)
harm or inconvenience.


Level 3 Possession, control, production or supply of data with the intent to
commit any of the above computer offences.


Level 4 Accessing data that is subject to an access control restriction

















Ref: https://www.legislation.gov.au/Details/C2004A00937
                                                                                                                                          27

28

      What   ar e  cyber secur i t y  and    i nfor mat i on  secur i t y?


      These are umbrella     terms. They are the processes, people, tools and
      techniques used to combat cybercrime.


      Key goals include ensuring:
      Confidentiality-   (also known as secrecy), meaning that the information
      assets can be accessed and disclosed only by authorised            parties
      Integrity- meaning that the information assets can only be modified or
      deleted by authorised      parties in authorised    ways, therefore they are
      always complete and true
      Availability- meaning that the information assets are accessible to the
      authorised   parties in a timely manner


      …amongst others

29
     Wher e  ar e  t he  mai n  poi nt s  of  vul ner abi l i t y?

30
      What   ar e  t hr ee  key  ar eas  t o  focus  on?











































      (Baltzan, Lynch & Fisher 2015, p.244)

Is cybersecurity for you?


Cybersecurity is one of the biggest growing areas of employment for information systems
professionals


Organisations    are more digitally transformed now, meaning they are more exposed than ever to
cybercrime


They need whole-of-organization risk and cybersecurity plans. This work might be completed by
In-House or Consulting agencies.


There is a significant lack of experts in this area, in both Australia and internationally, to create
and implement these strategies.

Summary
Cybersecurity, ethics and privacy
